title: Measures of Anchoring in Estimation Tasks

item_level_results.judgment: 75 of 92 Ss judged BGBBBB to be less likely than GBGBBG (p < .01 by sign test).
Ss viewed BBBGGG as significantly less likely than GBBGBG (p < .01).

high_school_programs.results: 67 of 89 Ss guessed that the class belongs to program A (p < .01 by sign test). Note: Objectively, program B is slightly more likely.

replicability_of_significance.statistical_note: A realistic estimate is lower than .50.

marble_distribution.results: 36 of 52 Ss viewed distribution I as more probable than distribution II (p < .01 by sign test). Note: Objectively, II is more probable.

sampling_distribution_tasks.summary_results: Sample size had no effect whatsoever on the subjective sampling distributions. Independent groups produced indistinguishable distributions across N = 10, 100, and 1000 for all three populations (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c).

ordinal_sample_size_judgments.summary: The modal answer was 'same' in almost all comparisons. There was no significant preference for the correct answer in any problem.

symmetric_binomial_posterior.summary_results: Population proportions (p) had no effect on subjective posterior estimates. Estimates depended on sample ratio (r:b) rather than sample difference (r-b). Median posterior estimates were very close to the stated sample proportion.

height_posterior_odds.judgment: A significant majority (86 of 115) assigned a higher value to the single observation (case i) than the sample of 6 (case ii), violating the correct ordering of likelihoods.
